# @andes/form

Form allows the user to input data, and is a system defined by the relationship between several form controls and their criteria of use.

The UI specs are defined in _[Andes UI](http://andes.adminml.com)_.

## How to use

### Installation

> Follow the [general installation guide](../../docs/installation.md).

```bash
npm install --save @andes/form
```

### Styles

> Follow the [general styles usage guide](../../docs/styles-usage.md) for configuration and how-tos.

Import the component in your bundle:

```scss
@import '@andes/form/index';
```

```scss
@import '@andes/form/lib/styles/helper/index';
@import '@andes/badge/index';
```

### Scripts

> Follow the [general scripts usage guide](../../docs/scripts-usage.md) for configuration and how-tos.

Import the component in your page:

```jsx
import { Form, Helper } from '@andes/form';
```

## Examples

For full demo and configuration options, check out the [story for Form](https://andes.adminml.com/web/?path=/story/form--andes-form).

`Form` should wrap other form control components, such as `TextField`, `Dropdown`, `Checkbox` and `RadioButton`.

```jsx
<Form>
  <TextField label="Name" />
</Form>
```

## Props

| Property | Description | Type | Default | Required |
| -------- | ----------- | ---- | ------- | -------- |
| `children` | Custom content. Ideally other form control components, such as `TextField`, `Dropdown`, `Checkbox` and `RadioButton`. | node |  | ✅ |
| `className` | Custom class. | string | `''` |  |

## CSRF protection by default

This component automatically renders an input hidden to integrate with Nordic's CSRF protection by default if you render ClientSide, if you only render ServerSide use [`AndesContext`](../context/README.md#andesprovider-api) to send CSRF token.
So this

```jsx
<Form>
  <TextField label="Name" />
</Form>
```

will render to this

```jsx
<Form>
  <input type="hidden" name="_csrf" value={someToken} />
  <TextField label="Name" />
</Form>
```

## Accessibility

Due to accessibility and validation issues, whenever an error occurs in a form field, it should focus on the first element that appears with an error so that the user acknowledges it and can start navigating through each to fix them. An example of how to control it was added to the Storybook. [with error validation example](https://andes.adminml.com/web/?selectedKind=Form)

## Examples

For full demo and configuration options, check out the [story for Helper](https://andes.adminml.com/web/?path=/story/helper--helper-single-text).

```jsx
  <fieldset>
    <legend>Selected option</legend>

    <Checkbox
      label="Option 1"
      value="1"
      inputProps={{ 'aria-describedby': 'checkbox-helper' }}
    />
    <Checkbox
      label="Option 2"
      value="2"
      inputProps={{ 'aria-describedby': 'checkbox-helper' }}
    />
    <Checkbox
      label="Option 3"
      value="3"
      inputProps={{ 'aria-describedby': 'checkbox-helper' }}
    />
  
    <Helper id="checkbox-helper">This is a helper text.</Helper>
  </fieldset>
```

### `<Helper>`

| Property | Description | Type | Default | Required |
| -------- | ----------- | ---- | ------- | -------- |
| `children` | Custom content. | `node` | | ✅ |
| `className` | Custom class. | `string` | `undefined` | |
| `id` | Unique identifier. | `string` | `undefined` | |
| `modifier` | Modifier to apply different styles of the Helper. | `string: 'error', 'caution'` | `undefined` | |

## Theming

This component supports theming. It allows you to pass a `customTheme` object to it adjusting colors, typography, spacing and styles to align with a brand's visual identity, user preferences or project requirements.

<br />

The `customTheme` object can make use of values ​​referenced in the component or CSS properties for quick customization or make use of style overrides according to `data-attributes` specific to the component for more complex cases.

<br />

### Using Referenced Component Values

Helper allows modifying the following attributes:

| Attribute                | Description                      |
| ------------------------ | -------------------------------- |
| `text`                   | Helper text color.               |

Taking this into account, the `customTheme` object has the following structure:

```jsx
const customTheme = {
  components: {
    helper: {
      color: {
        text: {
          [state]: textColorValue,
        },
      },
    },
  },
};
```

Possible `state` values:

```jsx

state: default | caution | error;

```

> For a more detailed view of the structure of this object you can refer to the file `@andes/themes/meli-themes/ml.json`

#### Example

<img src="resources/helper/docs/Helper-Example-Reference-Theming.png" alt="Reference Theming Helper Example" width="320" />

```jsx
import React from 'react';
import { Helper } from '@andes/form';
import { AndesThemeProvider } from '@andes/themes';
import './styles.scss';

const customTheme = {
  components: {
    helper: {
      color: {
        text: {
          caution: '#f73',
        },
      },
    },
  },
};

const MyComponent = () => (
  <AndesThemeProvider theme={customTheme}>
    <Helper modifier="caution">
      This can be a single or multiline text.
    </Helper>
  </AndesThemeProvider>
);

export default MyComponent;
```

```css
/* styles.scss */
@import '@andes/form/index';
```

> Remember that it is not necessary for the ThemeProvider to be next to the component you want to customize. Ideally it should be at the App level, wrapping your entire page.

### Using CSS Properties

The component allows passing CSS properties following this structure:

```jsx
const customTheme = {
  components: {
    helper: {
      [cssProperty]: propertyValue,
    },
  },
};
```

> Please note that styles applied in this way will impact all Andes helper regardless of their variant. Additionally, they have higher precedence than styles applied using referenced component values.

>[!WARNING]
> In this component the CSS styles will be applied to the container of the helper, since it has a composed layout CSS properties functionality may not work for your use case.

### Using `styleOverrides`

This component has a series of `data-attributes` that serve as a hook to style complex cases:

#### Basic button - data-attributes

| data-attribute                | Description                                                |
| ----------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- |
| `data-andes-helper`           | Helper container tag.                                      |
| `data-andes-state`            | Helper container state tag. Can be: `'caution'` or `'error'`.    |
| `data-andes-helper-badge`     | Helper badge tag.                                          |
| `data-andes-helper-label`     | Helper text tag.                                           |

Taking advantage of the them, you can use an entry called `styleOverrides` where you can add styles according to any `data-attribute`.

#### Example

<img src="resources/helper/docs/Helper-Example-Style-Overrides-Theming.png" alt="Style Overrides Theming Helper Example" width="320" />

```jsx
import React from 'react';
import { Helper } from '@andes/form';
import { AndesThemeProvider } from '@andes/themes';
import './styles.scss';

const customTheme = {
  components: {
    helper: {
      styleOverrides: {
        '[data-andes-helper-badge] > [data-andes-badge]': {
          borderRadius: '3px',
        },
      },
    },
  },
};

const MyComponent = () => (
  <AndesThemeProvider theme={customTheme}>
    <Helper modifier="error">
      This can be a single or multiline text.
    </Helper>
  </AndesThemeProvider>
);

export default MyComponent;
```

```css
/* styles.scss */
@import '@andes/form/index';
```

> Remember that it is not necessary for the ThemeProvider to be next to the component you want to customize. Ideally it should be at the App level, wrapping your entire page.
